sources.original_notes: Domestic flights within UK
sources.methodology_notes: Includes radiative forcing (RF) multiplier. 'Average passenger' class. DEFRA recommends including RF for company reporting. The WITHOUT-RF value is approximately 60% of this number.

air_travel_domestic.key_insight: Only one source covers this activity (UK Government GHG Conversion Factors). No cross-source comparison is possible, which is itself a finding — it means there is no independent validation available.

global.rationale: No single source covers all geographies. Use DEFRA for EU operations (includes RF), EPA for US operations (excludes RF). Document the choice and RF treatment explicitly.

sources.original_notes: US region. Distance: >= 2300 miles. Does NOT include radiative forcing.
sources.methodology_notes: GHG Protocol sources US air travel from DEFRA via EPA. Does NOT include radiative forcing. Converted from passenger-miles to passenger-km.

variance.diagnosis: Radiative forcing (RF) inclusion. DEFRA includes RF (accounting for non-CO2 climate effects of aviation like contrails and NOx at altitude), which roughly increases the climate impact by 50-90%. EPA and GHG Protocol do not include RF. This is a genuine methodological choice, not an error.
drivers: system_boundary
geographic
methodological

decomposition.note: ~100% of variance from system boundary differences, ~0% from other factors (geography, methodology, data vintage)

uncertainty.notes: Range across 3 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

air_travel_long_haul.key_insight: These 3 sources vary by 42.6%. Radiative forcing (RF) inclusion. DEFRA includes RF (accounting for non-CO2 climate effects of aviation like contrails and NOx at altitude), which roughly increases the climate impact by 50-90%. EPA and GHG Protocol do not include RF. This is a genuine methodological choice, not an error.

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
sources.original_notes: Aluminium cans and foil, excluding forming. Primary material production.
sources.methodology_notes: Embodied carbon — primary material production (no recycling credit). DEFRA also publishes a 'closed-loop' value that nets out recycled content. This is for specific product forms (cans/foil), not raw material.

uncertainty.notes: Primary vs recycled content, production process variation (e.g., BF-BOF vs EAF for steel), and system boundary choices.

aluminum.key_insight: Only one source covers this activity (UK Government GHG Conversion Factors). No cross-source comparison is possible, which is itself a finding — it means there is no independent validation available.

effective_independence.note: Single source — no independent validation available.

csrd_eu.rationale: DEFRA provides embodied carbon factors for common materials. For CSRD, also consider ecoinvent or PEFCR product-specific factors.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA does not publish materials embodied carbon factors. DEFRA is the most accessible public source.

global.rationale: Single-source coverage. For higher-stakes reporting, supplement with lifecycle databases (ecoinvent, GaBi).

decision_framework.defensibility_note: Single source — no independent validation available.

aluminum.quick_guidance: Single source available. Consider supplementing with industry-specific LCA data for material accuracy.

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)

variance.diagnosis: Geographic fleet composition. US and UK vehicle fleets differ in average vehicle size, fuel efficiency standards, fuel mix, and public transit ridership patterns. US buses tend to show lower per-passenger-km emissions partly due to different occupancy assumptions.
drivers: geographic

uncertainty.notes: Range across 3 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

bus.key_insight: These 3 sources vary by 98.7%. Geographic fleet composition. US and UK vehicle fleets differ in average vehicle size, fuel efficiency standards, fuel mix, and public transit ridership patterns. US buses tend to show lower per-passenger-km emissions partly due to different occupancy assumptions.

effective_independence.note: GHG Protocol compiles from EPA. Effective independence: 2 of 3 listed sources.

csrd_eu.rationale: EU-aligned publisher with detailed UK/EU vehicle categories. For non-UK EU operations, consider country-specific factors where available.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA factors reflect US fleet composition, fuel efficiency standards, and driving patterns. GHG Protocol values may use older EPA vintages — check data currency.

global.rationale: Ground transport factors are highly geography-dependent (fleet composition, fuel mix, occupancy). Always match the factor's geographic scope to the reporting entity's operations.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: GHG Protocol compiles from EPA. Effective independence: 2 of 3 listed sources.

bus.quick_guidance: US and UK factors measure different fleets. Use EPA for US operations, DEFRA for UK. Do not average them.

sources.original_unit: lb CO2 per MWh (total output, US average)
sources.original_notes: eGRID2023 total output emission factor. US national average across all subregions. CH4 and N2O converted to CO2e using AR5 GWPs.
sources.methodology_notes: eGRID2023 total output emission factor. US national average across all subregions. CH4 and N2O converted to CO2e using AR5 GWPs.

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
sources.original_notes: UK national grid average, generation only (excludes T&D losses)
sources.methodology_notes: UK national grid average. T&D losses are reported separately under Scope 3. This is generation-only.

variance.diagnosis: Geographic grid mix. US electricity (eGRID2023) reflects a coal/gas-heavy grid averaging ~0.35 kg CO2e/kWh, while UK electricity reflects a grid that has rapidly decarbonized (offshore wind, nuclear) to ~0.18 kg CO2e/kWh. This is not a methodology difference — it's a real physical difference in how electricity is generated.
drivers: geographic

uncertainty.notes: Range across 2 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

electricity_grid.key_insight: These 2 sources vary by 66.1%. Geographic grid mix. US electricity (eGRID2023) reflects a coal/gas-heavy grid averaging ~0.35 kg CO2e/kWh, while UK electricity reflects a grid that has rapidly decarbonized (offshore wind, nuclear) to ~0.18 kg CO2e/kWh. This is not a methodology difference — it's a real physical difference in how electricity is generated.

effective_independence.note: All 2 sources are independently derived.

csrd_eu.rationale: CSRD requires location-based Scope 2 using country or regional grid factors. Ember provides the most current annual data. National averages are acceptable; subregional is better.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA eGRID provides US subregional factors — more accurate than national average. SEC and CDP both accept eGRID as authoritative for US operations.

global.rationale: Always use the most geographically specific grid factor available. National > continental > global. Update annually — grid factors have the shortest shelf life of any emission factor.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: All 2 sources are independently derived.

electricity_grid.quick_guidance: Use eGRID for US location-based Scope 2. Use DEFRA for UK. For market-based: use supplier-specific RECs/EACs.

sources.original_notes: Medium- and Heavy-Duty Truck. Converted from vehicle-miles to vehicle-km.
sources.methodology_notes: Vehicle-mile basis (not ton-mile). For weight-distance, see row 425.

uncertainty.notes: Payload utilization, route efficiency, vehicle age, and per-tonne-km vs per-vehicle-km basis choices.

freight_road_truck.key_insight: Only one source covers this activity (EPA GHG Emission Factors Hub). No cross-source comparison is possible, which is itself a finding — it means there is no independent validation available.

effective_independence.note: Single source — no independent validation available.

csrd_eu.rationale: DEFRA provides granular freight categories (articulated vs rigid, laden vs average). EU reporting benefits from this specificity.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA provides US-specific truck freight factors. Match to the specific vehicle type where possible.

global.rationale: Freight factors vary significantly by vehicle type, load factor, and road conditions. Use the most specific factor available for each operation.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: Single source — no independent validation available.

freight_road_truck.quick_guidance: Use region-matched source. EPA for US trucking, DEFRA for UK. GHG Protocol may use stale underlying data.

sources.original_notes: CO2 only; CH4 and N2O added during normalization using AR5 GWPs
sources.methodology_notes: Higher Heating Value (HHV) basis. IPCC AR5 GWPs (CH4=28, N2O=265). CH4 and N2O converted to CO2e and added.

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
sources.original_notes: Net Calorific Value (LHV) basis. Already in kg CO2e.
sources.methodology_notes: Net Calorific Value (Lower Heating Value) basis. Note: EPA uses Gross/HHV which gives ~10% higher energy content per unit fuel.

sources.original_notes: CO2 only. CH4/N2O in separate table. LHV (Net CV) basis.
sources.methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 default factor. Lower Heating Value (Net CV) basis. CO2 only — does not include CH4/N2O from combustion.

variance.diagnosis: Energy measurement basis. EPA uses Higher Heating Value (HHV/Gross CV), while DEFRA and IPCC use Lower Heating Value (LHV/Net CV). HHV includes the latent heat of water vapor in combustion products; LHV does not. The difference is ~10% for natural gas. Additionally, the IPCC factor (via GHG Protocol) is CO2-only while EPA/DEFRA include CH4/N2O.
drivers: system_boundary
geographic
methodological

decomposition.note: ~47% of variance from system boundary differences, ~53% from other factors (geography, methodology, data vintage)

uncertainty.notes: Range across 3 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

natural_gas_stationary.key_insight: These 3 sources vary by 11.0%. Energy measurement basis. EPA uses Higher Heating Value (HHV/Gross CV), while DEFRA and IPCC use Lower Heating Value (LHV/Net CV). HHV includes the latent heat of water vapor in combustion products; LHV does not. The difference is ~10% for natural gas. Additionally, the IPCC factor (via GHG Protocol) is CO2-only while EPA/DEFRA include CH4/N2O.

effective_independence.note: GHG Protocol compiles from IPCC. Effective independence: 2.5 of 3 listed sources.

csrd_eu.rationale: Natural gas combustion chemistry is universal. All sources agree within ~11%, driven by HHV vs LHV measurement basis. Use DEFRA (LHV) for consistency with EU energy reporting conventions.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA uses HHV (Higher Heating Value), which aligns with US energy reporting conventions. The difference vs LHV is ~10% — not trivial but well-understood.

global.rationale: IPCC provides globally accepted default factors. Be explicit about HHV vs LHV basis — this is the main source of apparent disagreement.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: GHG Protocol compiles from IPCC. Effective independence: 2.5 of 3 listed sources.

natural_gas_stationary.quick_guidance: EPA uses HHV; DEFRA uses LHV. Match to your energy metering. Difference is ~10%, purely methodological.

sources.original_notes: Vehicle type: Passenger Car. Converted from vehicle-miles to vehicle-km.
sources.methodology_notes: Mixed fleet average. CO2 only in original; CH4/N2O added using AR5 GWPs.

sources.original_notes: US Passenger Car. Converted from vehicle-miles to vehicle-km.
sources.methodology_notes: From EPA via GHG Protocol. WARNING: GHG Protocol value (0.175 kg CO2/mi) differs significantly from EPA Hub value (0.297 kg CO2/mi). Likely different fleet definitions or data vintages. The EPA Hub 2025 value appears to be a newer, higher estimate. This 51% discrepancy is itself a key finding.

variance.diagnosis: Data vintage. GHG Protocol Cross-Sector Emission Factors uses older underlying data. Emission factors change as energy grids evolve, vehicle fleets turn over, and measurement methodologies improve.
drivers: methodological

uncertainty.notes: Range across 2 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

passenger_car.key_insight: These 2 sources vary by 51.7%. Data vintage. GHG Protocol Cross-Sector Emission Factors uses older underlying data. Emission factors change as energy grids evolve, vehicle fleets turn over, and measurement methodologies improve.

effective_independence.note: GHG Protocol compiles from EPA. Effective independence: 1 of 2 listed sources.

csrd_eu.rationale: EU-aligned publisher with detailed UK/EU vehicle categories. For non-UK EU operations, consider country-specific factors where available.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA factors reflect US fleet composition, fuel efficiency standards, and driving patterns. GHG Protocol values may use older EPA vintages — check data currency.

global.rationale: Ground transport factors are highly geography-dependent (fleet composition, fuel mix, occupancy). Always match the factor's geographic scope to the reporting entity's operations.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: GHG Protocol compiles from EPA. Effective independence: 1 of 2 listed sources.

passenger_car.quick_guidance: GHG Protocol data is stale. Use EPA for US, DEFRA for UK. Check vintage before reporting.

sources.original_notes: Intercity Rail - National Average

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)

variance.diagnosis: Geographic infrastructure. UK rail is partially electrified with higher occupancy rates. US intercity rail (Amtrak) covers longer distances with lower occupancy, affecting per-passenger-km intensity.
drivers: geographic

uncertainty.notes: Range across 2 independent sources. True uncertainty may be wider.

rail_intercity.key_insight: These 2 sources vary by 51.6%. Geographic infrastructure. UK rail is partially electrified with higher occupancy rates. US intercity rail (Amtrak) covers longer distances with lower occupancy, affecting per-passenger-km intensity.

effective_independence.note: All 2 sources are independently derived.

csrd_eu.rationale: EU-aligned publisher with detailed UK/EU vehicle categories. For non-UK EU operations, consider country-specific factors where available.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA factors reflect US fleet composition, fuel efficiency standards, and driving patterns. GHG Protocol values may use older EPA vintages — check data currency.

global.rationale: Ground transport factors are highly geography-dependent (fleet composition, fuel mix, occupancy). Always match the factor's geographic scope to the reporting entity's operations.

decision_framework.defensibility_note: All 2 sources are independently derived.

rail_intercity.quick_guidance: UK rail is partly electrified with higher occupancy. Use geographic-matched factors. Do not cross-apply.

sources.source_publisher: Department for Energy Security and Net Zero (DESNZ)
sources.original_notes: Steel cans — primary material production. Not raw steel.
sources.methodology_notes: Embodied carbon — primary material production (no recycling credit). DEFRA also publishes a 'closed-loop' value that nets out recycled content. This is for specific product forms (cans/foil), not raw material.

uncertainty.notes: Primary vs recycled content, production process variation (e.g., BF-BOF vs EAF for steel), and system boundary choices.

steel.key_insight: Only one source covers this activity (UK Government GHG Conversion Factors). No cross-source comparison is possible, which is itself a finding — it means there is no independent validation available.

effective_independence.note: Single source — no independent validation available.

csrd_eu.rationale: DEFRA provides embodied carbon factors for common materials. For CSRD, also consider ecoinvent or PEFCR product-specific factors.

sec_cdp_us.rationale: EPA does not publish materials embodied carbon factors. DEFRA is the most accessible public source.

global.rationale: Single-source coverage. For higher-stakes reporting, supplement with lifecycle databases (ecoinvent, GaBi).

decision_framework.defensibility_note: Single source — no independent validation available.

steel.quick_guidance: Single source. Supplement with World Steel Association data for production-route specificity.

countries.methodology_notes: Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. China grid mix: ~60% coal, ~16% hydro, ~16% wind/solar, ~5% nuclear. Coal dominance drives high intensity.

countries.source_name: Ember Global Electricity Review / Fraunhofer ISE
countries.methodology_notes: Estimated from Ember country profile (2023: 371 g/kWh) adjusted for 2024 renewables exceeding 60% of generation per Fraunhofer ISE. Germany grid mix: ~46% renewables, ~26% coal, ~15% gas, ~12% nuclear phase-out.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. India grid mix: ~75% coal, ~12% renewables, ~3% nuclear. Highest intensity among the 5 countries.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Russia grid mix: ~47% gas, ~20% hydro, ~18% nuclear, ~15% coal. Moderate intensity due to hydro/nuclear share.
Ember 2025 Global Electricity Review, 2024 data. Generation-based (not consumption-based). Includes lifecycle CO2e. US grid mix: ~40% gas, ~18% coal, ~21% nuclear, ~21% renewables.

electricity_grid.key_insight: These 5 countries vary by 69.8% in grid carbon intensity. India (1.9x Germany) reflects real differences in electricity generation mix: India's grid is ~75% coal, while Germany has exceeded 60% renewables. This is not a methodology difference — it's a physical difference in how electricity is generated. Implication: the same kWh of electricity has very different climate impact depending on where it's consumed. A company's Scope 2 emissions are heavily geography-dependent.

US.notes: Driven by coal-to-gas switching and wind/solar growth. 2021 uptick from post-COVID demand rebound.

CN.notes: Slow decline despite massive renewable buildout, offset by rising absolute demand. Coal still ~60% of generation.

IN.notes: Modest decline. Coal remains ~75% of generation. Solar growing fast but from small base.

DE.notes: Fastest decline. Energiewende driving coal phase-out, 60%+ renewables by 2024. 2022 uptick from gas crisis/coal restart.

RU.notes: Relatively low baseline due to large hydro/nuclear share. Slight increase in recent years from gas generation growth.

countries.methodology_notes: IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. Natural gas composition is uniform worldwide. China-specific data not separately published. NCV/LHV basis.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Germany uses UBA national factors operationally, but IPCC default shown here for cross-country consistency.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. India does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.
IPCC 2006 Guidelines global default. NCV/LHV basis. Russia does not publish a separate national natural gas emission factor.

IPCC 2006 Guidelines Vol 2, Ch 2, Table 2.2. Global default — natural gas composition is relatively uniform worldwide (uncertainty range: 54,200-58,300 kg CO2/TJ). NCV/LHV basis. Applied as US factor since IPCC provides a single global default for natural gas.

natural_gas_stationary.key_insight: All 5 countries show identical factors (0% variance) because the IPCC provides a single global default for natural gas. Unlike electricity or transport, natural gas composition is relatively uniform worldwide — the combustion chemistry doesn't vary by country. This is itself a key finding: some emission sources are genuinely geography-independent. The real country-level variation in natural gas emissions comes from upstream methane leakage rates (not captured here), which can vary 2-10x between countries.

countries.methodology_notes: ICCT new car fleet average 129 g CO2/km (NEDC, 2021) adjusted +20% for real-world gap. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated). China's BEV/PHEV share was ~35% of new sales in 2023, driving rapid fleet improvement. CAUTION: new-sales weighted, on-road fleet average is higher.

UBA TREMOD model 2022. Includes all car types (petrol 165, diesel 173 g/pkm). Occupancy: 1.4 persons/vehicle (German national average). This is the most transparent national transport emission factor database among the 5 countries.

ICCT new car fleet average 121.9 g CO2/km (MIDC cycle, FY2018-19) adjusted +20% for real-world driving gap. Occupancy: 1.6 (higher than Western average, estimated). Indian cars are predominantly smaller/lighter than US/EU fleet.
Derived from IEA fleet average fuel consumption (8.3 Lge/100km, 2019). Russia has no fuel economy standards. ~16% above global average per IEA. Post-2019 data unavailable. Occupancy: 1.5 (estimated).
EPA on-road fleet average (22.2 mpg, 8,887 g CO2/gallon). Occupancy: 1.5 persons/vehicle (US DOT 2019). On-road fleet includes all ages, not just new sales. Real-world driving.

passenger_car_gasoline.key_insight: These 5 countries vary by 57.3% (Germany is 1.8x India). Variance is driven by three factors: (1) vehicle fleet composition — Indian/Chinese fleets are smaller and lighter than US/German fleets; (2) fuel efficiency standards — EU regulations are stricter than US; (3) occupancy assumptions — ranging from 1.4 (Germany) to 1.6 (India). China's rapid BEV/PHEV adoption (~35% of new sales in 2023) is also pulling fleet averages down. Caution: some values use new-sales averages while others use on-road fleet averages, which can differ by 20-40%. Data quality caveat: India, Russia use stale data (2+ years old), so these figures may not reflect current conditions.

countries.source_name: Academic study (Beijing bus smart card data)
countries.methodology_notes: Beijing-specific study using smart card ridership data. Chinese urban buses have higher occupancy than US/EU. CAUTION: Beijing data only, not nationally representative. Tier-2/3 cities may differ significantly. No national aggregate available.

UBA TREMOD model 2022. Scheduled bus average across urban/suburban routes. Diesel buses dominate at 96 g/pkm. Long-distance coaches are much lower (31 g/pkm) due to higher occupancy. German average occupancy embedded in TREMOD calculations.

countries.source_name: WRI India-Specific Road Transport Emission Factors
countries.methodology_notes: WRI 2015 India-specific factors. Extremely low per-pkm because Indian urban buses operate at very high occupancy (60-80+ passengers, often standing room). Per vehicle-km, Indian diesel buses emit comparably to other countries. The low per-pkm is an occupancy effect, not a technology effect.

No Russia-specific bus emission factor exists in any publicly available source. This is an IPCC regional default for Eastern Europe/CIS, derived from typical diesel bus fuel consumption (30-35 L/100 vkm) and estimated average occupancy. Russia does not systematically report transport emission factors.

CBO 2022 analysis of US bus emissions. All bus types (transit + intercity coach). US transit buses have low average occupancy (~25% capacity), inflating per-pkm. Intercity coaches have higher occupancy. Blended average.

bus.key_insight: These 5 countries vary by 136.1% (United States is 7.3x India). This variance is driven almost entirely by occupancy, not technology. Indian urban buses operate at extremely high occupancy (60-80+ passengers, often standing room), producing just 15 g CO2/passenger-km. US transit buses average ~25% capacity utilization, inflating per-passenger emissions to 110 g/km. Per vehicle-km, all countries' diesel buses emit comparably (~800-1200 g CO2/vkm). The per-passenger-km metric amplifies ridership differences. Data quality caveat: India, Russia use stale data (2+ years old), so these figures may not reflect current conditions.

subregions.subregion_name: ERCOT All (Electric Reliability Council of Texas)

subregion_comparison.key_insight: Within the US alone, grid carbon intensity varies by 147.9% across 28 eGRID subregions (plus CAISO). PRMS (Puerto Rico Miscellaneous) is 6.4x more carbon-intensive than NYUP (NPCC Upstate NY). This means a company's Scope 2 emissions depend heavily on which US subregion they operate in. Using the national average can over- or under-estimate by 2-3x.

nodes.label: DEFRA/DESNZ
nodes.publisher: Dept for Environment, Food & Rural Affairs (DEFRA)

summary.narrative: GHG Protocol provides no independently derived emission factors in its Cross-Sector Tools. Every factor traces back to EPA, DEFRA, or IPCC. Apparent 'three-source agreement' is circular citation.

useeio_sector.methodology_notes: Supply-chain model based on BEA input-output tables combined with EPA satellite accounts. Covers Scope 1+2+3 upstream emissions for the entire air transportation sector, averaged across all revenue.

conversion_bridge.cost_source: Bureau of Transportation Statistics, average domestic yield 2023
conversion_bridge.notes: Derived as: 0.51 kg/USD × $0.18/pkm = 0.0918 kg/pkm. Highly sensitive to fare assumptions — premium fares would lower the derived factor per dollar.

drivers: USEEIO is a top-down macro model that averages emissions across all revenue in the air transport sector, including cargo, charter, and ground operations
Activity-based factor (DEFRA) isolates passenger combustion emissions with radiative forcing uplift
The assumed fare/km is a national average — actual fares vary 2-4x by route and booking class
gap_analysis.interpretation: The 2.5x gap reflects fundamentally different measurement approaches. Spend-based captures a broader economic footprint but at lower precision. Use activity-based when you have passenger-km data.

guidance.when_to_use_spend: Scope 3 Category 6 (Business Travel) screening when you only have total airfare spend from expense reports, not itinerary-level data.
guidance.when_to_use_activity: Whenever you have flight itineraries with origin-destination pairs or distance data. Activity-based factors are 5-10x more accurate for aviation.
guidance.key_caveat: Never mix spend-based and activity-based factors for the same travel category to avoid double-counting.

useeio_sector.sector_name: Electric power generation, transmission, and distribution
useeio_sector.methodology_notes: Covers full supply chain of the electric power sector including generation, T&D losses, and upstream fuel extraction. Averaged across all electricity revenue.

conversion_bridge.cost_source: EIA average US retail electricity price, 2023
conversion_bridge.notes: Derived as: 4.67 kg/USD × $0.125/kWh = 0.584 kg/kWh. Commercial rates (~$0.11) vs residential (~$0.16) change this significantly.

drivers: USEEIO includes upstream fuel extraction, equipment manufacturing, and T&D infrastructure — activity-based EPA factor covers generation-only emissions
USEEIO factor reflects full supply chain (Scope 1+2+3 of the utility), while EPA eGRID is combustion at the plant
Electricity price varies 2-3x by state and rate class, making the conversion bridge sensitive
gap_analysis.interpretation: Surprisingly close — the 1.7x gap is smaller than most sectors because electricity generation is already the dominant emission source. The spend-based number is higher because it includes supply chain overhead.

guidance.when_to_use_spend: Scope 3 Category 1 screening for purchased electricity services when you only have utility bills in dollar amounts, not kWh consumption.
guidance.when_to_use_activity: Always for Scope 2 reporting — use EPA eGRID or utility-specific factors with actual kWh. Spend-based is never appropriate for Scope 2.
guidance.key_caveat: Spend-based electricity factors are Scope 3 tools. Scope 2 must always use location-based or market-based activity factors per GHG Protocol Scope 2 Guidance.

useeio_sector.sector_name: Iron and steel mills and ferroalloy manufacturing
useeio_sector.methodology_notes: Covers all GHG emissions in the iron and steel manufacturing supply chain, including coking, blast furnace operation, and upstream mining. Averaged across all steel products and revenue.

conversion_bridge.cost_source: World Steel Association, average US hot-rolled coil price, 2023
conversion_bridge.notes: Derived as: 1.42 kg/USD × $750/tonne = 1065 kg/tonne. Steel prices are highly volatile — can range from $400 to $1,500/tonne depending on grade and market conditions.

drivers: DEFRA factor is for steel cans specifically (higher processing intensity) while USEEIO covers all iron and steel mill output including lower-intensity products
Steel price volatility means the conversion bridge can shift 2x year-over-year
USEEIO averages across recycled and virgin steel production routes; DEFRA factor may reflect a specific mix
gap_analysis.interpretation: The 2.7x gap is driven by product specificity — DEFRA measures cans (a finished product), while USEEIO averages across the entire iron & steel sector. For procurement screening, spend-based is acceptable; for product-level LCA, use activity-based.

guidance.when_to_use_spend: Scope 3 Category 1 screening when you only know total steel procurement spend, not tonnage or product grade.
guidance.when_to_use_activity: When you have weight-based procurement data (tonnes of specific steel products). DEFRA or worldsteel factors are more accurate for specific products.
guidance.key_caveat: Steel prices fluctuate 50-100% year-over-year. Spend-based estimates from 2021 vs 2023 could differ 2x for identical physical quantities.

useeio_sector.methodology_notes: Covers all trucking sector emissions including long-haul, local delivery, and specialized freight. Averaged across all trucking revenue regardless of distance or cargo type.

conversion_bridge.cost_source: American Transportation Research Institute, average trucking operational cost per mile, 2023 (converted to km)
conversion_bridge.notes: Derived as: 0.82 kg/USD × $1.85/vkm = 1.517 kg/vkm. Costs vary significantly by truck class, load factor, and route type.

drivers: USEEIO includes full supply chain — vehicle manufacturing, maintenance, infrastructure, and upstream fuel — while EPA covers combustion only
Trucking cost per km varies 3x between local delivery and long-haul routes
Empty return trips inflate cost-per-km without proportional emissions, distorting the spend-based conversion
gap_analysis.interpretation: The 1.9x gap is modest and directionally expected — spend-based captures supply chain overhead. For logistics screening with invoice data, spend-based is a reasonable first pass.

guidance.when_to_use_spend: Scope 3 Category 4 (Upstream Transportation) or Category 9 (Downstream Transportation) when you only have freight invoices, not tonne-km or vehicle-km data.
guidance.when_to_use_activity: When your logistics provider gives you vehicle-km, tonne-km, or fuel consumption data. Activity-based is always preferred for transport.
guidance.key_caveat: Freight spend includes margins, surcharges, and accessorial fees that don't correlate with emissions. Strip fuel surcharges if possible before applying spend-based factors.

useeio_sector.methodology_notes: Covers all accommodation sector emissions including hotels, motels, B&Bs, and RV parks. Includes energy, water, laundry, food service, and upstream supply chain. Averaged across all accommodation revenue.

conversion_bridge.cost_source: STR Global, average US hotel ADR (average daily rate), 2023
conversion_bridge.notes: Derived as: 0.35 kg/USD × $150/night = 52.5 kg/night. Hotel rates vary 5x+ from budget motels ($60) to luxury hotels ($300+), making this highly sensitive.

drivers: USEEIO includes full supply chain — food service, laundry, amenities, construction amortization — while DEFRA covers operational energy only
Hotel room rates vary 5x+ by location and tier, making the cost bridge extremely sensitive
DEFRA factor is UK-specific where grid carbon intensity is ~50% lower than US average, explaining part of the gap
gap_analysis.interpretation: The 3.3x gap is expected — spend-based captures the full hospitality supply chain while activity-based covers only on-site energy. For business travel screening, spend-based is common but imprecise.

guidance.when_to_use_spend: Scope 3 Category 6 (Business Travel) screening when you only have hotel expense totals from corporate travel reports.
guidance.when_to_use_activity: When you have room-night counts. Even the simpler DEFRA per-night factor is more stable than spend-based, since it removes price variation.
guidance.key_caveat: A $300 luxury hotel night does not produce 2x the emissions of a $150 business hotel. Spend-based factors penalize premium accommodation disproportionately.
